title: Appoint accountable owners (security, quality, privacy)
explanation: Regulators and auditors expect named, accountable owners before any technical control is credited.
actions: Designate a security officer, a management representative for the QMS, and a data-protection lead.
Document responsibilities, escalation paths and management-review cadence.
Record executive sign-off and review at least annually.

title: Establish a quality management system (QMS)
explanation: A QMS with design controls, CAPA and document control is a precondition for nearly every device market.
actions: Implement ISO 13485 (and QMSR-aligned) procedures: design controls, CAPA, complaints, document control.
Run internal audits and management reviews on a defined schedule.
Maintain the Device Master Record and Design History File.

title: Maintain a unified control catalogue (crosswalk)
explanation: One control set mapped to many regimes lets you implement and evidence once, then comply many.
actions: Adopt a common control catalogue and map each control to applicable regulations.
Maintain the crosswalk and review when regulations change.
Operate a test-once/comply-many evidence model.

title: Conduct lifecycle risk management (ISO 14971)
explanation: Risk analysis, control and residual-risk acceptance are mandatory before market placement and recurring thereafter.
actions: Define a risk management plan and hazard analysis (FMEA/FTA).
Implement and verify risk controls; evaluate residual risk vs benefit.
Feed post-market data back into the risk file.

title: Security risk assessment (data + cyber)
explanation: Security risk analysis is explicitly required (HIPAA, GDPR Art 35, IEC 62443, MDCG 2019-16) and drives prioritisation.
actions: Inventory data flows and assets; identify threats and vulnerabilities.
Score likelihood and impact; record in a risk register.
Define a remediation roadmap with owners and dates.

title: Software lifecycle process (IEC 62304)
explanation: A defined software lifecycle with classification and verification is referenced by FDA and EU MDR submissions.
actions: Classify software (Class A/B/C) and document the development plan.
Maintain requirements, architecture, unit/integration/system test records.
Track SOUP/third-party components with versions.

title: Generate and maintain an SBOM
explanation: An SBOM is now mandatory for US cyber devices (524B) and expected under the EU CRA and MDCG guidance.
actions: Produce an SBOM (CycloneDX or SPDX) in the build pipeline.
Monitor components for new vulnerabilities continuously.
Publish/share the SBOM as required by each market.

title: Secure development lifecycle & code scanning
explanation: Secure-by-design and SAST/DAST/SCA gates satisfy CRA, 62443 SDL and SOC 2 change management.
actions: Add SAST, DAST and dependency (SCA) scanning to CI/CD.
Enforce code review and secrets scanning on the main branch.
Gate releases on security findings.

title: Coordinated vulnerability disclosure (CVD)
explanation: A published CVD policy and response process is a minimum artefact for FDA 524B, CRA and NIS2.
actions: Publish a vulnerability disclosure policy and intake channel.
Define triage, remediation SLAs and advisories.
Report actively-exploited vulnerabilities where required (CRA/NIS2).

title: Identity, MFA and least-privilege access
explanation: Access control is the most-cited safeguard across frameworks and the leading breach root-cause.
actions: Enforce SSO + MFA on all accounts; remove long-lived keys.
Implement least-privilege RBAC and periodic access reviews.
Log and retain access events.

title: Encrypt data at rest and in transit
explanation: Encryption/pseudonymisation satisfies HIPAA technical safeguards, GDPR Art 32 and PCI requirements.
actions: Enable encryption for all stores and transport (TLS, KMS-managed keys).
Manage keys with rotation and least-privilege access.
Document the approach for audit.

title: Data classification, retention and deletion
explanation: Knowing where regulated data lives and applying retention/deletion is foundational to privacy regimes.
actions: Classify data and map flows (incl. special-category/health).
Define retention schedules and secure deletion.
Apply DLP and access monitoring on sensitive stores.

title: Records of Processing & data-subject rights
explanation: GDPR-specific obligations (RoPA, DSAR, DPIA) carry direct supervisory-authority enforcement.
actions: Maintain Records of Processing Activities (Art 30).
Operate a data-subject-rights workflow (access/erasure/portability).
Run DPIAs for high-risk processing (Art 35).

title: Processor agreements (BAA / DPA)
explanation: You are accountable for processors: HIPAA BAAs and GDPR Art 28 DPAs are mandatory and audited.
actions: Execute BAAs (US/PHI) and DPAs (EU) with all processors.
Maintain a sub-processor register and due-diligence records.
Review vendors on a defined cycle.

title: Centralised logging and monitoring
explanation: Continuous monitoring and anomaly detection are required by SOC 2 CC7, HIPAA audit controls and NIS2.
actions: Aggregate logs into a SIEM with anomaly alerting.
Define alert triage SLAs and on-call.
Retain logs per regulatory minimums.

title: Incident response & breach notification
explanation: A tested IR capability with HIPAA 60-day, GDPR 72-hour and NIS2 reporting paths is mandatory.
actions: Document an IR plan with a breach classification matrix.
Build notification workflows (HIPAA 60-day, GDPR 72-hour, NIS2).
Run an annual tabletop and keep regulator contacts ready.

title: Vulnerability & patch management
explanation: Timely detection and patching is required by SOC 2 CC7.1, CRA and post-market cyber obligations.
actions: Scan infrastructure and container images on a schedule.
Set patch SLAs (e.g. critical 48h / high 14d).
Track in ticketing and run periodic penetration tests.

title: Backup, continuity and disaster recovery
explanation: Availability and recoverability are HIPAA contingency, GDPR Art 32 resilience and SOC 2 Availability requirements.
actions: Define RTO/RPO; automate and test backups (immutable where possible).
Design multi-AZ/region and document a DR runbook.
Run an annual DR test.

title: Segment OT/ICS networks (zones & conduits)
explanation: IEC 62443 and NIST 800-82 require segmentation between IT and OT to limit blast radius.
actions: Define zones and conduits; segment OT from IT and the internet.
Apply least-privilege firewalling and unidirectional gateways where needed.
Inventory OT assets and protocols.

title: OT monitoring and secure remote access
explanation: Continuous OT monitoring and brokered remote access reduce ICS compromise risk.
actions: Deploy OT-aware monitoring/IDS.
Broker and MFA all remote OT access.
Patch OT with tested maintenance windows.

title: Process & production controls
explanation: Validated, controlled production processes are required by QMS and manufacturing standards.
actions: Validate critical processes and maintain process controls.
Implement traceability and nonconformance handling.
Calibrate and maintain equipment on schedule.

title: Worker safety & environmental controls
explanation: OSHA, ISO 45001 and ISO 14001 require documented safety and environmental management on the shop floor.
actions: Conduct hazard assessments; implement machine guarding and PPE.
Maintain hazard communication and incident reporting.
Track environmental aspects and compliance obligations.

title: AI governance and data governance
explanation: AI as a device safety component is high-risk under the EU AI Act; ISO 42001 and FDA expect lifecycle governance.
actions: Inventory AI use; define an AI risk-management process.
Govern training/validation/test datasets and document model cards.
Implement human oversight and output audit trails.

title: AI security: no regulated data in prompts; testing
explanation: Prevent leakage of PHI/personal data into models and test for prompt-injection.
actions: Set a data-handling policy (no PHI/PII in prompts without controls).
Test for prompt injection and unsafe outputs.
Log model access and retain evidence.

title: Unique device identification (UDI)
explanation: UDI labelling and database submission are required in the US (GUDID) and EU (EUDAMED).
actions: Assign UDI-DI/UDI-PI and apply to labels.
Submit to GUDID (US) and EUDAMED (EU).
Keep UDI records current through changes.

title: Electronic records & audit trails (Part 11)
explanation: Trustworthy e-records with audit trails and validation are required for FDA-regulated software.
actions: Implement tamper-evident audit trails and access controls.
Validate systems (IQ/OQ/PQ) and manage e-signatures.
Retain records per requirements.

title: SOC 2 Type II readiness
explanation: Customers increasingly require a SOC 2 Type II report; it operationalises the Trust Services Criteria over a period.
actions: Scope Trust Services Criteria and the observation period.
Automate evidence collection and monitor operating effectiveness.
Engage an independent auditor and prepare the system description.

title: Post-market surveillance & vigilance
explanation: PMS, PMCF and adverse-event reporting are required by MDR, UK MDR and most device regimes.
actions: Operate a PMS system and PMCF plan.
Report incidents/vigilance to the relevant authority.
Feed findings into risk and design updates.

title: Design controls & Design History File
explanation: Design inputs/outputs, verification, validation and a DHF are mandatory QMS evidence for device approval.
actions: Define design inputs and trace to outputs and V&V.
Hold and document design reviews at each stage.
Maintain the Design History File and Device Master Record.

title: CAPA & complaint handling
explanation: Corrective/preventive action and complaint handling are core QMS processes auditors always examine.
actions: Operate a CAPA process with root-cause analysis and effectiveness checks.
Log, triage and investigate complaints within defined timelines.
Link complaints to vigilance and risk where applicable.

title: Document & record control
explanation: Controlled documents and retained records underpin every QMS and electronic-records requirement.
actions: Implement version-controlled SOPs with approval workflows.
Define record retention and protect against loss/alteration.
Control distribution and obsolescence of documents.

title: Internal audit & management review
explanation: Scheduled internal audits and management reviews are explicit QMS clauses and MDSAP audit areas.
actions: Run a risk-based internal audit programme.
Hold management reviews covering quality objectives and metrics.
Track findings to closure.

title: Clinical evaluation & evidence
explanation: Clinical evaluation and (for high-risk) clinical investigation evidence are required by MDR/IVDR and FDA.
actions: Plan and document a clinical evaluation per MEDDEV/MDR.
Maintain a clinical evaluation report and update it from PMCF.
Justify equivalence or generate clinical data as needed.

title: Labelling, IFU & EUDAMED/GUDID registration
explanation: Compliant labelling, instructions-for-use and database registration are market-access prerequisites.
actions: Produce labelling and IFU meeting each market's requirements.
Register devices in GUDID (US) and EUDAMED (EU).
Control label changes through the QMS.

title: Supplier qualification & purchasing controls
explanation: Qualified suppliers and purchasing controls are required by device QMS and manufacturing standards.
actions: Qualify and risk-tier suppliers; keep an approved supplier list.
Define purchasing controls and incoming inspection.
Monitor supplier performance and re-evaluate periodically.

title: Process validation & sterilization
explanation: Validated processes (incl. sterilization where relevant) are mandatory where output cannot be fully verified.
actions: Validate special processes (IQ/OQ/PQ); revalidate on change.
Validate sterilization and packaging where applicable.
Maintain process monitoring and statistical controls.

title: Traceability, lot control & recall readiness
explanation: Lot traceability and a field-safety/recall process are required for timely corrective actions in the field.
actions: Implement lot/serial traceability through production and distribution.
Define a recall / field safety corrective action procedure.
Test recall effectiveness periodically.

title: Human factors & usability engineering
explanation: Use-related risk must be controlled via usability engineering (IEC 62366) and is reviewed by FDA and Notified Bodies.
actions: Conduct formative and summative usability evaluations.
Identify use errors and mitigate via design.
Document the usability engineering file.

title: Electrical safety, EMC & essential performance
explanation: Type-testing to IEC 60601 (safety, EMC, essential performance) is required for medical electrical equipment.
actions: Plan and conduct IEC 60601 type testing.
Address EMC and environmental collateral standards.
Maintain test reports in the technical file.

title: Secure boot, firmware integrity & cryptography
explanation: Device integrity (secure boot, signed firmware, strong crypto) is central to FDA 524B, CRA and IEC 62443.
actions: Implement secure boot and signed firmware updates.
Use vetted cryptography and managed keys/certificates.
Protect debug/maintenance interfaces.

title: OT asset inventory & vulnerability management
explanation: An accurate OT asset inventory and tailored patching are foundational to NIST 800-82 and IEC 62443.
actions: Maintain a live OT/ICS asset and software inventory.
Assess vulnerabilities with OT-safe methods.
Patch within tested maintenance windows; compensate where patching is infeasible.

title: AI model validation, bias & performance monitoring
explanation: High-risk AI requires validated performance, bias evaluation and ongoing monitoring under the EU AI Act and FDA guidance.
actions: Validate model performance on representative data; evaluate bias.
Document intended use, limits and a model card.
Monitor live performance and drift; define update governance.

title: Security, privacy & quality training
explanation: Workforce training and awareness are explicit requirements across HIPAA, GDPR, ISO 27001 and device QMS.
actions: Deliver role-based security, privacy and GxP/QMS training.
Track completion and competency.
Refresh training on policy or regulatory change.

title: International data transfers & residency
explanation: Cross-border transfer mechanisms (SCCs, adequacy) and residency controls are required for EU personal data.
actions: Map cross-border data flows and apply transfer mechanisms (SCCs/adequacy).
Complete transfer impact assessments where needed.
Apply data-residency controls where required by market.

title: Multi-tenant isolation & data segregation
explanation: SaaS platforms must guarantee that one customer (tenant) cannot access another's data; isolation is a top concern in security reviews, SOC 2 and FedRAMP.
actions: Enforce tenant isolation at the data, application and network layers.
Scope every query and authorization check to the tenant; test for cross-tenant access.
Segregate or encrypt tenant data with per-tenant keys where feasible.

title: Accessibility conformance (WCAG / EN 301 549)
explanation: Digital products increasingly must meet accessibility standards; the EU Accessibility Act and US ADA web guidance make WCAG conformance a legal expectation.
actions: Adopt a WCAG 2.1/2.2 AA conformance target across the product.
Test with assistive technologies and automated + manual audits.
Publish an accessibility statement and remediate findings on a schedule.

title: Scope CUI and define the assessment boundary
explanation: CMMC and NIST 800-171 only credit controls inside a correctly drawn CUI boundary; a vague scope is the most common assessment failure.
actions: Identify every system, person and flow that stores, processes or transmits CUI.
Draw and diagram the assessment boundary (CUI enclave) and isolate it from the rest of the network.
Document the System Security Plan (SSP) describing the boundary and how each control is met.

title: FIPS-validated cryptography for CUI
explanation: NIST 800-171 (3.13.11) and CMMC require FIPS 140-validated modules to protect CUI - ordinary TLS is not sufficient evidence on its own.
actions: Use FIPS 140-2/3 validated cryptographic modules for CUI at rest and in transit.
Record the CMVP certificate numbers for each module in the SSP.
Disable non-validated cipher suites and legacy protocols.

title: SPRS score, POA&M and assessment readiness
explanation: DoD contracts require a current self-assessment score in SPRS and a managed POA&M; CMMC Level 2 adds a third-party (C3PAO) assessment.
actions: Run the NIST 800-171 self-assessment and post the score to SPRS.
Maintain a Plan of Action & Milestones (POA&M) for any unmet controls with owners and dates.
Prepare evidence and schedule the required C3PAO assessment for the contract's CMMC level.

title: Defense incident reporting & media protection
explanation: DFARS requires reporting cyber incidents affecting CUI to DoD within 72 hours; media handling and sanitisation are explicit 800-171 control families.
actions: Build a 72-hour DoD/DC3 incident-reporting workflow for incidents touching CUI.
Mark, store and transport CUI media under the media-protection controls; sanitise before reuse or disposal.
Preserve and protect incident images and malware artefacts as required by DFARS.

title: Scope and segment the cardholder data environment (CDE)
explanation: PCI DSS applies to everything in or connected to the CDE; network segmentation is the single biggest lever for reducing scope, cost and risk.
actions: Map all cardholder data flows, storage and processing to define the CDE.
Segment the CDE from the rest of the network and validate segmentation at least annually (six-monthly for service providers).
Minimise stored cardholder data and never store sensitive authentication data after authorisation.

title: External (ASV) scanning & penetration testing
explanation: PCI DSS requires quarterly external vulnerability scans by an Approved Scanning Vendor and annual segmentation-aware penetration testing.
actions: Run quarterly ASV scans and remediate to a passing scan.
Perform internal and external penetration tests at least annually and after significant change.
Test segmentation controls as part of the penetration test.

title: Anti-malware, change-detection & secure payment pages
explanation: PCI DSS v4.0 strengthened protection of payment pages (e.g. script and change-/tamper-detection) and requires anti-malware on at-risk systems.
actions: Deploy and maintain anti-malware on systems commonly affected by malware.
Manage and monitor all scripts on payment pages and detect unauthorised changes to page headers/content.
Use change- and file-integrity monitoring to alert on unexpected modifications in the CDE.

title: Targeted risk analyses & customised approach (v4.0.1)
explanation: PCI DSS v4.0 lets entities tailor how some requirements are met via a documented targeted risk analysis - now a mandatory, future-dated activity.
actions: Perform a documented targeted risk analysis for each requirement that allows one (e.g. frequency-based tasks).
Document any customised-approach controls and the assurance evidence supporting them.
Review and refresh targeted risk analyses at least every 12 months.
